description: Holmes and Watson are close friends who regularly discuss cases and share a living/working space.
context: had called upon my friend Sherlock Holmes... I seated myself in his armchair

description: Holmes is familiar with Peterson, a commissionaire who found the hat and goose and brought them to him.
context: You know Peterson, the commissionaire? ... It is to him that this trophy belongs.

description: Holmes is actively trying to identify and locate Henry Baker, the owner of the lost hat and goose.
context: Its owner is unknown... I continue to retain the hat of the unknown gentleman who lost his Christmas dinner.

description: Holmes previously attempted to recover papers belonging to Irene Adler.
context: You allude to my attempt to recover the Irene Adler papers

description: Holmes previously investigated a case involving Miss Mary Sutherland.
context: to the singular case of Miss Mary Sutherland

target_name: The Man with the Twisted Lip
description: Holmes previously investigated an adventure involving the man with the twisted lip.
context: and to the adventure of the man with the twisted lip.

description: Watson is aware of Miss Mary Sutherland through Holmes's past cases.
context: three have been entirely free of any legal crime... You allude to... the singular case of Miss Mary Sutherland

description: Peterson found the battered hat on the street after a fight broke out.
context: He found it... left in possession of the field of battle, and also of the spoils of victory in the shape of this battered hat

description: Peterson found the goose dropped by the stranger during the altercation.
context: dropped his goose... left in possession of the field of battle, and also of the spoils of victory in the shape of this battered hat and a most unimpeachable Christmas goose.

description: Peterson was walking homeward down Tottenham Court Road on Christmas morning.
context: making his way homeward down Tottenham Court Road.

description: Peterson reached the corner of Goodge Street where the altercation occurred.
context: As he reached the corner of Goodge Street, a row broke out between this stranger and a little knot of roughs.

description: Peterson intervened in the street fight to protect the stranger and recovered the lost items.
context: Peterson had rushed forward to protect the stranger from his assailants... left in possession of the field of battle

description: Henry Baker is the legal owner of the hat, as indicated by the initials on the lining and the card.
context: For Mrs. Henry Baker was printed upon a small card... initials 'H. B.' are legible upon the lining of this hat

description: Henry Baker is the owner of the goose, as indicated by the card tied to its leg.
context: For Mrs. Henry Baker was printed upon a small card which was tied to the bird's left leg

description: Henry Baker was the stranger who lost his hat and goose during the street altercation.
context: a tallish man... carrying a white goose... knocked off the man's hat... dropped his goose, took to his heels, and vanished

description: Holmes deduces that Henry Baker's decline in fortunes and habits are triggered by moral retrogression, likely due to drink.
context: pointing to a moral retrogression, which, when taken with the decline of his fortunes, seems to indicate some evil influence, probably drink, at work upon him.

source_name: The Man with the Twisted Lip
description: The stranger (identified as the man with the twisted lip) fled into the back streets of Tottenham Court Road after the fight.
context: vanished amid the labyrinth of small streets which lie at the back of Tottenham Court Road.

source_name: The Man with the Twisted Lip
description: The man with the twisted lip was the stranger involved in the street fight that caused the loss of the hat and goose.
context: a row broke out between this stranger and a little knot of roughs. One of the latter knocked off the man's hat, on which he raised his stick to defend himself

description: Holmes uses the hat as a physical object for deduction and intellectual analysis.
context: I beg that you will look upon it not as a battered billycock but as an intellectual problem. Here is my lens. You know my methods.

description: Holmes expresses his intellectual methods through the practice of deduction.
context: You know my methods. What can you gather yourself as to the individuality of the man who has worn this article?

description: Holmes's deductive process involves analyzing physical evidence to determine the identity and circumstances of Henry Baker.
context: On the contrary, Watson, you can see everything. You fail, however, to reason from what you see. You are too timid in drawing your inferences.

Deduces the hat owner's physical traits, financial decline, and marital strife from the hat's condition and markings.
context: He has, however, retained some degree of selfrespect,' he continued, disregarding my remonstrance. 'He is a man who leads a sedentary life, goes out little, is out of training entirely, is middleaged, has grizzled hair which he has had cut within the last few days, and which he anoints with limecream. These are the more patent facts which are to be deduced from his hat.

description: Analyzes the hat's physical characteristics to extract clues about its owner's lifestyle and habits.
context: For answer Holmes clapped the hat upon his head. It came right over the forehead and settled upon the bridge of his nose. 'It is a question of cubic capacity,' said he; 'a man with so large a brain must have something in it.'

description: Identifies self-respect as evidenced by the owner's attempt to conceal stains with ink rather than discarding the hat.
context: he has endeavoured to conceal some of these stains upon the felt by daubing them with ink, which is a sign that he has not entirely lost his self-respect.

description: Notes a decline in foresight, indicated by a broken hat-securer elastic that was never replaced.
context: he has broken the elastic and has not troubled to replace it, it is obvious that he has less foresight now than formerly, which is a distinct proof of a weakening nature.

description: Employs a lens to examine hair ends, dust, and moisture on the hat's lining to confirm physical traits.
context: The lens discloses a large number of hair-ends, clean cut by the scissors of the barber. They all appear to be adhesive, and there is a distinct odour of lime-cream. This dust, you will observe, is not the gritty, grey dust of the street but the fluffy brown dust of the house

description: Carries the goose home as a peace-offering to his wife.
context: Nay, he was bringing home the goose as a peaceoffering to his wife. Remember the card upon the bird's leg.

description: Rushes into Holmes's apartment to report a significant discovery regarding the goose.
context: Peterson, the commissionaire, rushed into the apartment with flushed cheeks and the face of a man who is dazed with astonishment.

description: Finds a blue stone inside the crop of the goose brought by his wife.
context: See here, sir! See what my wife found in its crop! He held out his hand and displayed upon the centre of his palm a brilliantly scintillating blue stone

description: Discovers the Blue Carbuncle diamond hidden within the goose and presents it to Holmes.
context: displayed upon the centre of his palm a brilliantly scintillating blue stone, rather smaller than a bean in size, but of such purity and radiance that it twinkled like an electric point in the dark hollow of his hand.

description: Recognizes the stone as the unique Blue Carbuncle and knows its market value and the offered reward.
context: It's more than a precious stone. It is the precious stone... I ought to know its size and shape, seeing that I have read the advertisement about it in The Times every day lately.

description: Regularly reads advertisements about the Blue Carbuncle in The Times to track the gem.
context: I ought to know its size and shape, seeing that I have read the advertisement about it in The Times every day lately.

description: Is the rightful owner of the Blue Carbuncle diamond, which was lost at the Hotel Cosmopolitan.
context: Not the Countess of Morcar's blue carbuncle!' I ejaculated. 'Precisely so. I ought to know its size and shape, seeing that I have read the advertisement about it in The Times every day lately.

description: Understands the Countess's sentimental motivations and willingness to pay a high reward to recover the gem.
context: I have reason to know that there are sentimental considerations in the background which would induce the Countess to part with half her fortune if she could but recover the gem.

description: Classifies the discovery of the diamond as a classic case of treasure trove.
context: this is treasure trove indeed. I suppose you know what you have got?

description: Is accused of stealing the Blue Carbuncle from the Countess's jewel-case.
context: John Horner, a plumber, was accused of having abstracted it from the lady's jewel-case. The evidence against him was so strong that the case has been referred to the Assizes.

description: The Assizes are handling the case against John Horner due to strong evidence of theft.
context: The evidence against him was so strong that the case has been referred to the Assizes.

description: Conducts the deductive analysis of the hat to determine the owner's characteristics.
context: These are the more patent facts which are to be deduced from his hat. Also, by the way, that it is extremely improbable that he has gas laid on in his house.

description: Discovers the diamond in the goose and brings it to Holmes for identification.
context: See here, sir! See what my wife found in its crop! He held out his hand and displayed upon the centre of his palm a brilliantly scintillating blue stone

description: Identifies the discovered stone as the Blue Carbuncle and explains its significance and value.

properties.contribution: identifies gem and explains its significance

context: It's more than a precious stone. It is the precious stone... I ought to know its size and shape, seeing that I have read the advertisement about it in The Times every day lately.

description: Investigates the theft of the Blue Carbuncle and reviews newspaper accounts of the case.

properties.contribution: reviews case details and newspaper reports

context: John Horner, a plumber, was accused of having abstracted it from the lady's jewel-case. The evidence against him was so strong that the case has been referred to the Assizes. I have some account of the matter here, I believe.

description: The Assizes are adjudicating the case regarding the theft of the Blue Carbuncle due to strong evidence.
context: The evidence against him was so strong that the case has been referred to the Assizes.

description: Engages in a dialogue with Watson, explaining deductions and responding to his skepticism and confusion.
context: You are certainly joking, Holmes.' 'Not in the least. Is it possible that even now, when I give you these results, you are unable to see how they are attained?' 'I have no doubt that I am very stupid, but I must confess that I am unable to follow you.

Horner is charged with stealing the Countess's jewel, implying a fraudulent or criminal act against her property.
context: abstracted from the jewel-case of the Countess of Morcar the valuable gem known as the blue carbuncle.

description: Ryder, as an upper-attendant, escorted Horner to the Countess's dressing-room to perform repairs.
context: shown Horner up to the dressing-room of the Countess of Morcar upon the day of the robbery in order that he might solder the second bar of the grate

description: Cusack serves as the personal maid to the Countess.
context: Catherine Cusack, maid to the Countess, deposed to having heard Ryder's cry of dismay

description: Bradstreet is an inspector assigned to the B Division of the police force.
context: Inspector Bradstreet, B division, gave evidence as to the arrest of Horner

description: Holmes takes on the case to solve the sequence of events surrounding the jewel theft.
context: The question for us now to solve is the sequence of events leading from a rifled jewel-case at one end to the crop of a goose in Tottenham Court Road at the other.

description: Holmes seeks to determine Henry Baker's involvement in the mystery surrounding the stolen gem.
context: So now we must set ourselves very seriously to finding this gentleman and ascertaining what part he has played in this little mystery.

description: Watson collaborates with Holmes on the case, sharing deductions and assisting in the investigation.
context: You see, Watson, our little deductions have suddenly assumed a much more important and less innocent aspect.

description: Peterson is tasked by Holmes to place an advertisement in the evening papers.
context: Here you are, Peterson, run down to the advertising agency and have this put in the evening papers.

description: Holmes takes possession of the recovered gem to secure it and study it.
context: I shall keep the stone. Thank you.

description: Holmes uses his personal strong box to store the valuable Blue Carbuncle.
context: I'll lock it up in my strong box now and drop a line to the Countess to say that we have it.

description: Holmes intends to notify the Countess that the stolen gem has been recovered.
context: drop a line to the Countess to say that we have it.

description: Watson questions Holmes regarding the moral standing and guilt of John Horner.
context: Do you think that this man Horner is innocent? I cannot tell.

description: Holmes assesses Henry Baker's character and involvement, leaning toward his innocence.
context: It is, I think, much more likely that Henry Baker is an absolutely innocent man, who had no idea that the bird which he was carrying was of considerably more value than if it were made of solid gold.

description: Watson returns to Holmes's residence in the evening to observe the case resolution.
context: I had been delayed at a case, and it was a little after half-past six when I found myself in Baker Street once more.

description: Henry Baker claims ownership of the black felt hat found with the goose.
context: Yes, sir, that is undoubtedly my hat.

description: Holmes lives and operates his detective practice from this address.
context: applying at 6.30 this evening at 221b, Baker Street.

description: Ryder works and lives at the Hotel Cosmopolitan as an upper-attendant.
context: James Ryder, upper-attendant at the hotel, gave his evidence

description: Horner is the defendant in the police court trial regarding the jewel theft.
context: Horner, who had shown signs of intense emotion during the proceedings, fainted away at the conclusion and was carried out of court.

description: The Assizes are tasked with determining the legal guilt or innocence in the case.
context: the magistrate refused to deal summarily with the offence, but referred it to the Assizes.

description: Holmes reviews the police court proceedings to understand the official narrative and evidence.
context: Hum! So much for the police-court,' said Holmes thoughtfully, tossing aside the paper.

description: Holmes examines the gem closely, noting its physical properties and criminal history.
context: When the commissionaire had gone, Holmes took up the stone and held it against the light. 'It's a bonny thing,' said he.

description: Watson shares his professional duties and case updates with Holmes, relying on his partnership.
context: In that case I shall continue my professional round. But I shall come back in the evening at the hour you have mentioned, for I should like to see the solution of so tangled a business.

description: Henry Baker travels to Baker Street in response to the advertisement to claim his lost items.
context: Mr. Henry Baker can have the same by applying at 6.30 this evening at 221b, Baker Street.

description: Holmes questions Henry Baker directly to verify his story and assess his innocence.
context: I am at a loss to know now why you did not advertise.

description: Holmes acquires a goose to replace the one Peterson's family consumed, intending to use it for the investigation.
context: And, I say, Peterson, just buy a goose on your way back and leave it here with me, for we must have one to give to this gentleman in place of the one which your family is now devouring.

description: The Morocco Casket is used to store the Blue Carbuncle, linking the object to the crime scene.
context: the small morocco casket in which, as it afterwards transpired, the Countess was accustomed to keep her jewel, was lying empty upon the dressing-table.

description: The Blue Carbuncle originates from the banks of the Amoy River in southern China.
context: This stone is not yet twenty years old. It was found in the banks of the Amoy River in southern China

description: Holmes utilizes the Evening Papers as a medium to place an advertisement and attract Henry Baker.
context: these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers.

description: Holmes initiates the advertisement placement to solve the mystery and recover the goose.
context: To do this, we must try the simplest means first, and these lie undoubtedly in an advertisement in all the evening papers.

description: Watson accompanies Holmes to the meeting with Henry Baker to observe the case resolution.
context: Just as I arrived the door was opened, and we were shown up together to Holmes' room.

description: Holmes hosts and conducts the meeting with Henry Baker to verify his identity and story.
context: greeting his visitor with the easy air of geniality which he could so readily assume.

Baker acknowledges Holmes's assistance and expresses gratitude for the return of his property.
context: I am much indebted to you, sir, for a Scotch bonnet is fitted neither to my years nor my gravity.

description: Baker was the victim of an assault by a gang of roughs who stole his hat and goose.
context: I had no doubt that the gang of roughs who assaulted me had carried off both my hat and the bird.

description: Baker frequents the Alpha Inn as a regular patron and member of the local goose club.
context: There are a few of us who frequent the Alpha Inn, near the Museum-we are to be found in the Museum itself during the day, you understand.

description: Baker is a paying member of the Goose Club, which provides him with a bird at Christmas.
context: This year our good host, Windigate by name, instituted a goose club, by which, on consideration of some few pence every week, we were each to receive a bird at Christmas.

description: Baker owns the Scotch bonnet hat that was stolen during the assault.
context: I am much indebted to you, sir, for a Scotch bonnet is fitted neither to my years nor my gravity.

description: Holmes takes on the case to investigate the assault and theft involving Baker's property.
context: Then I suggest that we turn our dinner into a supper and follow up this clue while it is still hot.

description: Holmes leads the broader investigation into the origin of the geese and the underlying crime.
context: but we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police, and which a singular chance has placed in our hands.

description: Holmes collaborates closely with Watson, treating him as a companion and assistant in the case.
context: So much for Mr. Henry Baker,' said Holmes when he had closed the door behind him. 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter. Are you hungry, Watson?

description: Holmes visits the Alpha Inn to question the landlord about the goose supply chain.
context: In a quarter of an hour we were in Bloomsbury at the Alpha Inn, which is a small public-house at the corner of one of the streets which runs down into Holborn.

description: Holmes travels to Covent Garden Market to track down the salesman Breckinridge.
context: We passed across Holborn, down Endell Street, and so through a zigzag of slums to Covent Garden Market.

description: Holmes engages in a bet with Breckinridge regarding the origin of the geese.

properties.contribution: Staked a fiver on country-bred origin

context: If you won't tell us the bet is off, that is all. But I'm always ready to back my opinion on a matter of fowls, and I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred.

description: Watson accompanies Holmes to the Alpha Inn during their investigation.
context: In a quarter of an hour we were in Bloomsbury at the Alpha Inn, which is a small public-house at the corner of one of the streets which runs down into Holborn.

description: Watson follows Holmes to Covent Garden Market to observe the interrogation of Breckinridge.
context: We passed across Holborn, down Endell Street, and so through a zigzag of slums to Covent Garden Market.

description: Watson assists Holmes in the broader investigation by accompanying him and providing support.
context: Then I suggest that we turn our dinner into a supper and follow up this clue while it is still hot.

description: Windigate founded and operates the Goose Club, which supplies members with Christmas geese.
context: This year our good host, Windigate by name, instituted a goose club, by which, on consideration of some few pence every week, we were each to receive a bird at Christmas.

description: Windigate is the landlord and proprietor of the Alpha Inn.
context: Holmes pushed open the door of the private bar and ordered two glasses of beer from the ruddy-faced, white-aproned landlord.

description: Breckinridge operates a stall at Covent Garden Market as a salesman.
context: One of the largest stalls bore the name of Breckinridge upon it, and the proprietor a horseylooking man, with a sharp face and trim side-whiskers was helping a boy to put up the shutters.

description: Breckinridge accepts Holmes's bet regarding the geographical origin of the geese.

properties.contribution: Staked a sovereign on town-bred origin

context: 'Will you bet, then?' 'It is merely taking your money, for I know that I am right. But I'll have a sovereign on with you, just to teach you not to be obstinate.'

description: Bill assists Breckinridge as a young boy at his market stall.
context: The salesman nodded and shot a questioning glance at my companion. ... 'Bring me the books, Bill,' said he. The small boy brought round a small thin volume and a great greasy-backed one...

description: Baker is a key witness in the investigation, though he initially knows nothing of the underlying matter.

properties.contribution: Provides initial clue via stolen property

context: 'It is quite certain that he knows nothing whatever about the matter.

description: The police attempted to investigate the assault but missed the crucial line of inquiry.
context: but we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police, and which a singular chance has placed in our hands.

description: Baker originally owned the goose that was stolen during the assault.
context: I had no doubt that the gang of roughs who assaulted me had carried off both my hat and the bird.

description: Breckinridge maintains detailed ledgers tracking his suppliers and their accounts.
context: You see this little book?' 'Well?' 'That's the list of the folk from whom I buy. D'you see? Well, then, here on this page are the country folk, and the numbers after their names are where their accounts are in the big ledger.

description: The assault carries the legal consequence of seven years' penal servitude for the perpetrator.
context: we have at the other a man who will certainly get seven years' penal servitude unless we can establish his innocence.

description: The wager centers entirely on classifying whether the geese are country-bred or town-bred.
context: I have a fiver on it that the bird I ate is country bred." / "Well, then, you've lost your fiver, for it's town bred,

description: The investigation aims to establish the perpetrator's innocence or guilt, thereby resolving the moral and legal dilemma.
context: unless we can establish his innocence. It is possible that our inquiry may but confirm his guilt; but, in any case, we have a line of investigation which has been missed by the police...

Holmes collaborates with Watson as his companion and assistant during the investigation.
context: my companion speedily overtook the little man and touched him upon the shoulder.

description: Holmes deduces Ryder's identity and confronts him regarding the stolen gem.
context: I think that I could be of assistance to you. ... My name is Sherlock Holmes. It is my business to know what other people don't know.

description: Holmes plans to visit Mrs. Oakshott to trace the geese and the stolen carbuncle.
context: whether we should go on to this Mrs. Oakshott to-night, or whether we should reserve it for to-morrow.

description: Holmes observes Breckinridge arguing with Ryder at the market stall.
context: Breckinridge, the salesman, framed in the door of his stall, was shaking his fists fiercely at the cringing figure.

description: Holmes deduces that Windigate purchased the geese containing the stolen gem.
context: sold by Mrs. Oakshott, of Brixton Road, to a salesman named Breckinridge, by him in turn to Mr. Windigate, of the Alpha

context: It was Catherine Cusack who told me of it,

context: Give him an arm back into his chair, Watson. He's not got blood enough to go in for felony with impunity.

Holmes interrogates and assesses Ryder's criminal nature and motives.
context: 'I see-her ladyship's waiting-maid. Well, the temptation of sudden wealth so easily acquired was too much for you, as it has been for better men before you; but you were not very scrupulous in the means you used. It seems to me, Ryder, that there is the making of a very pretty villain in you.'

description: Ryder confesses his crimes and pleads for mercy to Holmes.
context: 'I will tell you it just as it happened, sir,' said he. 'When Horner had been arrested, it seemed to me that it would be best for me to get away with the stone at once, for I did not know at what moment the police might not take it into their heads to search me and my room.'

description: Ryder frames Horner for the jewel theft to divert suspicion.
context: 'You knew that this man Horner, the plumber, had been concerned in some such matter before, and that suspicion would rest the more readily upon him.'

description: Horner lives in fear of Ryder's accusations and the resulting arrest.
context: 'It is very well to cringe and crawl now, but you thought little enough of this poor Horner in the dock for a crime of which he knew nothing.'

description: Ryder hires or utilizes Cusack as an accomplice for the heist.
context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

description: Cusack acts as an accomplice to Ryder during the jewel robbery.
context: 'You made some small job in my lady's room-you and your confederate Cusack-and you managed that he should be the man sent for.'

description: Maggie is married to Oakshott and lives with him.
context: 'She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

description: Oakshott is the husband of Maggie Oakshott.
context: 'She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

description: Ryder knows Maggie as his sister and seeks her help to hide the stolen stone.
context: 'I went out, as if on some commission, and I made for my sister's house. She had married a man named Oakshott, and lived in Brixton Road, where she fattened fowls for the market.'

description: Maggie knows Ryder as her brother and provides him with geese.
context: 'My sister asked me what was the matter, and why I was so pale; but I told her that I had been upset by the jewel robbery at the hotel.'

description: Ryder knows Maudsley as a former criminal associate who can help fence the stone.
context: 'I had a friend once called Maudsley, who went to the bad, and has just been serving his time in Pentonville. One day he had met me, and fell into talk about the ways of thieves, and how they could get rid of what they stole.'

description: Maudsley knows Ryder as a friend and fellow thief.
context: 'I knew that he would be true to me, for I knew one or two things about him; so I made up my mind to go right on to Kilburn, where he lived, and take him into my confidence.'

description: Watson assists Holmes as his companion and chronicler during the investigation.

description: Holmes interrogates Ryder and listens to his confession.
